class_schedule.topic: What Is Learning? Behavioral and Cognitive Perspectives
learning_objectives: Define learning from behavioral and cognitive perspectives
Explain classical and operant conditioning with examples
Contrast behaviorist and cognitivist views of learning
readings: How Learning Works Ch. 1 by Ambrose et al.
Psychology of Learning and Motivation Ch. 1 (supplementary)

lecture_notes.introduction: Learning is a permanent change in behavior or knowledge that results from experience. But what counts as learning, and how it happens, has been debated for over a century. Understanding these theories makes you a more effective learner and, eventually, a more effective teacher, manager, or communicator.
key_concepts: Behaviorism: learning as observable behavior change (Watson, Skinner)
Classical conditioning: Pavlov's dog, stimulus-response associations
Operant conditioning: reinforcement (positive/negative), punishment, extinction
Cognitivism: learning as internal mental processing (Piaget, information processing)
The shift from 'what did they do?' to 'what did they understand?'
lecture_notes.demonstration: Identifying classical and operant conditioning in everyday life: advertising, education, pet training, and app design
setup_requirements: Examples handout of conditioning in daily life (provided)

review.value: Pairing a neutral stimulus with an unconditioned stimulus until the neutral stimulus alone triggers the response. Example: hearing a can opener (neutral) paired with food (unconditioned) makes a cat come running at the sound alone.

Behavior is shaped by its consequences. Positive reinforcement (reward) increases behavior. Negative reinforcement (removing something aversive) also increases behavior. Punishment decreases behavior.

class_schedule.topic: Memory: How We Encode, Store, and Retrieve Information
learning_objectives: Describe the multi-store model of memory: sensory, short-term, long-term
Explain encoding strategies that improve long-term retention
Identify common memory failures and their causes
readings: Make It Stick Ch. 1-2 by Brown, Roediger & McDaniel

lecture_notes.introduction: Memory is the foundation of learning. Without the ability to encode, store, and retrieve information, learning is impossible. Understanding how memory works reveals why some study strategies are effective and others are a waste of time.
key_concepts: Sensory memory: brief, raw sensory input (milliseconds to seconds)
Working memory: limited capacity (7±2 items), active processing
Long-term memory: unlimited capacity, organized by meaning and association
Encoding strategies: elaboration, organization, visualization, dual coding
Forgetting: decay, interference, retrieval failure, and the Ebbinghaus curve
lecture_notes.demonstration: Memory experiment: students attempt to memorize two lists, one using rote repetition and one using elaborative encoding, then compare recall after 10 minutes
setup_requirements: Memory experiment materials: two word lists (provided)

review.value: Working memory holds about 7±2 chunks of information. Chunking (grouping items into meaningful units) expands effective capacity. Teaching must respect this limit.
Without review, we forget approximately 50% of new information within an hour and 70% within 24 hours. Spaced retrieval practice dramatically flattens this curve.

class_schedule.topic: Effective Study Strategies: What Works and What Doesn't
learning_objectives: Distinguish between effective and ineffective study strategies based on research
Apply retrieval practice and spaced repetition to your own learning
Explain why some popular study habits (highlighting, rereading) are ineffective
readings: Dunlosky et al., Improving Students' Learning (research summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: Most students study in ways that feel productive but are actually ineffective. Research in cognitive psychology has identified which strategies produce durable learning and which create only the illusion of knowing.
key_concepts: Retrieval practice: testing yourself is the best way to learn, not just assess
Spaced repetition: distributing study over time beats cramming
Interleaving: mixing different topics or problem types improves transfer
The illusion of competence: fluency from rereading is not the same as understanding
Desirable difficulties: learning that feels harder often produces better retention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students test retrieval practice in real time: study a passage, then attempt free recall vs. rereading, and compare retention after 20 minutes
setup_requirements: Study passages for retrieval practice experiment (provided)

review.value: Every time you retrieve information from memory, you strengthen that memory trace. Flashcards, practice tests, and free recall are more effective than rereading or highlighting because they force retrieval.

Study strategies that feel easy (rereading, highlighting) create fluency illusions. Strategies that feel harder (testing, spacing, interleaving) produce better long-term retention. Difficulty is a signal of learning, not a sign of failure.

learning_objectives: Compare intrinsic and extrinsic motivation and their effects on learning
Apply self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, and relatedness
Explain Carol Dweck's growth mindset vs. fixed mindset research

lecture_notes.introduction: Motivation determines whether learning happens at all. A student who believes they can improve will study differently than one who believes ability is fixed. Understanding motivation science helps you sustain effort through difficulty.
key_concepts: Intrinsic motivation: driven by interest, curiosity, and personal satisfaction
Extrinsic motivation: driven by grades, rewards, or avoiding punishment
Self-determination theory: humans need autonomy, competence, and relatedness
Growth mindset: believing abilities can be developed through effort and strategy
Fixed mindset: believing abilities are innate and unchangeable
Attribution theory: how we explain success and failure affects future effort
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mindset self-assessment followed by guided analysis of how attributions for a recent academic outcome affected subsequent motivation

review.value: People are most motivated when three needs are met: autonomy (feeling of choice and control), competence (feeling effective and capable), relatedness (feeling connected to others). Environments that thwart these needs undermine motivation.

Students with a growth mindset see challenges as opportunities to improve and respond to failure with increased effort and new strategies. Fixed mindset students avoid challenges and interpret failure as proof of inability.

learning_objectives: Define metacognition and explain its three components: knowledge, monitoring, and regulation
Assess your own metacognitive accuracy using calibration exercises
Apply metacognitive strategies to improve learning effectiveness
readings: Metacognition in the College Classroom (supplementary article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Metacognition is the ability to think about your own thinking. It is the single strongest predictor of academic success, more powerful than raw intelligence or prior knowledge. Metacognitive learners know what they know, know what they do not know, and adjust their strategies accordingly.
key_concepts: Metacognitive knowledge: knowing your strengths, weaknesses, and effective strategies
Metacognitive monitoring: assessing your own comprehension in real time
Metacognitive regulation: adjusting strategies based on monitoring
Calibration: the alignment between confidence and actual performance
The Dunning-Kruger effect: low performers overestimate their competence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calibration exercise: students predict their quiz score before taking a practice quiz, then compare predictions to actual results
setup_requirements: Practice quiz with confidence calibration questions (provided)

review.value: Before studying: 'What do I already know? What do I need to learn? What strategy will I use?' During studying: 'Am I understanding this? Should I slow down?' After studying: 'Can I explain this without notes? What gaps remain?'

People with low ability in a domain tend to overestimate their competence because they lack the knowledge to recognize what they do not know. Expertise brings both ability and awareness of limitations.

class_schedule.topic: Transfer of Learning and Real-World Application
learning_objectives: Define transfer of learning and distinguish between near and far transfer
Identify conditions that promote transfer from academic settings to real-world contexts
Apply course concepts to design a personal learning plan

lecture_notes.introduction: The ultimate goal of education is not to pass tests but to apply what you learn in new contexts. Transfer of learning is the ability to take knowledge from one situation and use it effectively in another. It is the difference between education and mere training.
key_concepts: Near transfer: applying learning to similar contexts (practicing math problems, then taking a math test)
Far transfer: applying learning to very different contexts (using statistical reasoning to evaluate news claims)
Conditions promoting transfer: deep understanding, varied practice, explicit abstraction
Analogical reasoning: finding structural similarities between different domains
Deliberate practice: focused, feedback-rich practice targeting weaknesses
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students identify examples of transfer in their own academic and personal lives and analyze what enabled it

review.value: Surface learning (memorizing facts and procedures) enables near transfer. Deep learning (understanding underlying principles) enables far transfer. Asking 'why does this work?' promotes deep understanding.
Not just repetition but focused practice on specific weaknesses with immediate feedback. Expert performance in any domain requires thousands of hours of deliberate practice, not just experience.

questions.question: Explain the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve and describe two evidence-based study strategies that counteract forgetting. Why is rereading not an effective strategy?
questions.answer: The Ebbinghaus forgetting curve shows that without review, we forget approximately 50% of newly learned information within one hour, 70% within 24 hours, and 90% within a week. Two strategies that counteract forgetting: (1) Spaced repetition: reviewing material at increasing intervals (1 day, 3 days, 1 week, 2 weeks) strengthens memory traces each time and dramatically slows forgetting. (2) Retrieval practice: testing yourself (flashcards, free recall, practice quizzes) forces you to reconstruct information from memory, which strengthens the neural pathways for that information far more than passive review. Rereading is ineffective because it creates a fluency illusion: the material feels familiar when you see it, so you believe you know it, but recognition is not the same as recall. On an exam, you need to retrieve information from memory, and rereading does not practice retrieval.

rubric.key: Correct description of the forgetting curve with time estimates
Explains why rereading is ineffective (fluency illusion)

questions.question: Compare classical conditioning and operant conditioning. Give one real-world example of each that occurs in a college setting.
questions.answer: Classical conditioning involves learning associations between stimuli. A neutral stimulus is paired with a stimulus that naturally causes a response until the neutral stimulus alone triggers the response. It is passive and involuntary. Example: A student who had a panic attack during an exam (unconditioned stimulus causing anxiety) may later feel anxious just walking into the exam room (conditioned stimulus triggering conditioned response). Operant conditioning involves learning from the consequences of voluntary behavior. Behaviors followed by reinforcement increase; behaviors followed by punishment decrease. Example: A student who studies hard (behavior) and receives an A (positive reinforcement) is more likely to study hard in the future. A student who texts in class (behavior) and gets called out publicly (positive punishment) is less likely to text again. Key difference: classical conditioning is about automatic responses to paired stimuli; operant conditioning is about voluntary behaviors shaped by consequences.

rubric.key: Identifies the key difference between them

questions.question: Explain self-determination theory and describe how a college course could be designed to support all three psychological needs (autonomy, competence, relatedness).
questions.answer: Self-determination theory (Deci and Ryan) states that intrinsic motivation depends on three innate psychological needs: autonomy (feeling of choice and self-direction), competence (feeling effective and capable of growth), and relatedness (feeling connected to and valued by others). Course design supporting all three: Autonomy: allow students to choose their essay topic from a list or propose their own, and offer multiple assignment formats (paper, presentation, or project). Competence: provide clear rubrics before assignments so students know what success looks like, give timely feedback that identifies specific strengths and areas for improvement, and scaffold difficult skills with structured practice before high-stakes assessment. Relatedness: build in peer review partnerships, create discussion groups that stay consistent throughout the semester, and learn student names and acknowledge their contributions.

rubric.key: Correct definition of all three SDT needs
Specific course design feature for each need
Demonstrates understanding of how design affects motivation

questions.question: What is metacognition and why is it a better predictor of academic success than intelligence? Describe the three components of metacognition with an example of each.
questions.answer: Metacognition is the awareness and regulation of your own thinking and learning processes. It is a better predictor of academic success than intelligence because a student with high metacognition can identify what they do not know, choose effective strategies, and adjust when something is not working, regardless of raw cognitive ability. A highly intelligent student without metacognition may study ineffectively and overestimate their readiness. Three components: (1) Metacognitive knowledge: knowing your own learning strengths, weaknesses, and which strategies work for you. Example: 'I learn best by teaching material to someone else rather than just reading it.' (2) Metacognitive monitoring: assessing your comprehension in real time while studying. Example: 'I just read this page but I cannot summarize what it said, so I need to go back and read more carefully.' (3) Metacognitive regulation: adjusting your strategies based on monitoring. Example: 'My flashcard approach is not working for this concept; I am going to try drawing a diagram instead.'

rubric.key: Correct definition and importance of metacognition
All three components correctly named and defined

major_assignments.title: Personal Learning Science Experiment
major_assignments.description: Design and conduct a personal experiment testing one evidence-based learning strategy from the course in your actual study routine. Choose a strategy (retrieval practice, spaced repetition, interleaving, or elaborative interrogation), apply it systematically to material from another course, measure its effectiveness, and report your findings.
objectives: Apply learning science research to your own academic practice
Design a simple but rigorous personal experiment with measurable outcomes
Collect and analyze data on your own learning effectiveness
Reflect on how the experience changes your understanding of how you learn
deliverables: Experiment design document (due week 3): strategy chosen, hypothesis, method, and measurement plan
Data collection log: daily entries tracking study sessions, strategy use, and self-assessment
Results analysis (500 words) with data tables or charts
Written report (1000-1500 words) connecting your findings to course readings
Reflective conclusion (500 words) on what you learned about your own learning

criteria: Clear research question and hypothesis
Strategy chosen is evidence-based and correctly applied
Measurement method is reasonable and specific
Design allows comparison (before/after, strategy A vs. strategy B)
Consistent daily tracking over the experiment period
Data is specific and quantifiable where possible
Honest reporting of difficulties and deviations from plan
Sufficient data points to draw tentative conclusions
Data presented clearly with tables or charts
Results interpreted accurately without overclaiming
Connections to course readings are specific and meaningful
Limitations of the personal experiment acknowledged
Honest self-assessment of what was learned
Specific changes to study habits identified
Metacognitive awareness demonstrated
Well-written and thoughtful
Well-designed personal experiment applying evidence-based learning strategy
Consistent data collection with honest and thorough tracking
Thoughtful analysis connecting personal results to learning science research
Genuine reflection demonstrating growth in metacognitive awareness

quizzes.description: Assessment of learning psychology concepts and their application

weekly_learning_journals.description: Weekly reflections applying course concepts to your own study experiences

learning_experiment.description: Personal experiment testing an evidence-based learning strategy

class_participation.description: Active participation in discussions, demonstrations, and in-class experiments

final_exam.description: Comprehensive exam covering all learning psychology topics

course_policies.attendance: In-class demonstrations and experiments cannot be replicated outside of class. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce the participation grade to zero.
course_policies.late_submissions: Learning journals are due weekly. The experiment has staged deadlines. Late submissions lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 3 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussions and in-class experiments are collaborative. Written work, including the learning experiment, must be individual. You may discuss strategies with classmates but must collect your own data and write your own analysis.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Fabricating experimental data is the most serious form of academic dishonesty in a science course. All data must be genuinely collected from your own study experiences.
course_policies.technology_requirements: No specialized technology required. A notebook for learning journals and a spreadsheet for experiment data collection are sufficient.
